parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Welch Fowler, second wife of Samuel Newton

marriage_date.notes: Married early 1724; deed dated June 18, 1724 gives "Abner Newton of Milford and Mary his wife"

primary_residence.notes: Removed from Milford to Durham, settling there in late 1724. Deed of July 1724 calls him "Abner Newton of Milford"; deed of December 1724 calls him "Abner Newton, late of Milford, now of Durham."

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother shared same first name, Abner
narrative_blocks.body: This Abner Newton was born 1699 to Samuel Newton's second wife Sarah Welch Fowler — distinguishing him from his half-brother Abner Newton baptized May 1689, who had been born to Samuel's first wife Martha Fenn ten years earlier. The repeated Abner name across both marriages was uncommon. The 1699 Abner became the documented ancestor of the Durham Newton branch — earning the 'durham_newton' tag in this entry.

narrative_blocks.header: 1724 Milford-to-Durham move; wife sold inherited share
narrative_blocks.body: In 1724 Abner Newton and Mary Burwell removed from Milford to Durham Connecticut — and on the move Mary sold her own inherited property, a quarter share of a dwelling house and home-lot in Milford, to Susannah Burwell (widow of John Burwell) for eighty pounds. Per the deed boundaries this was the homestead of the first John Burwell, occupied since 1639. Mary had kept a share in her family's original Milford homestead until the day she left for Durham.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Roger received Alice Newton's Yale bequest
narrative_blocks.body: Abner's son Roger Newton was the beneficiary of Alice Newton's ca.1740 conditional Yale bequest — establishing the family's Yale-funded ministerial track. Roger graduated Yale 1758 and served fifty-six years as minister at Greenfield Massachusetts until 1816 — one of the longest continuous American pastorates of the Revolutionary-Republican era.

narrative_blocks.header: Lexington Alarm minute man at age forty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: He responded to the Lexington Alarm of April 1775 as a minute man in Capt. Jonas Lock's company, Col. Williams's Massachusetts regiment. He had been commissioned earlier to apprehend Tories in Durham, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Born twin — Greenfield reunion with brother Roger
narrative_blocks.body: He was twin to Abner Newton Jr., born together October 23, 1726 in the Durham Main Street house. After the Revolution he removed to Greenfield, Massachusetts, where his brother Rev. Roger Newton was minister for 53 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Hemlock swamps that became valuable land
narrative_blocks.body: He, his son Isaac, and his brother Roger purchased many acres of wild land in Greenfield at 7 shillings 6 pence per acre. The hemlock swamp later became valuable; he held many civic offices in the town after the war.

narrative_blocks.header: His second wife died two weeks before him
narrative_blocks.body: His second wife Hulda died September 14, 1802 in Greenfield, age 60. He died two weeks later on September 28, age nearly 76.

narrative_blocks.header: Born without a roof to his mouth
narrative_blocks.detail: His principal crop was rye; he spelled it 'ri.' His carefully kept account book, dated January 1, 1762, survived and was published in Caroline Newton's family history. The speech impediment was not only a social burden — it shaped his entire Revolutionary War service. Strangers could not understand him, and when he could not give the countersign intelligibly, he risked being shot by his own side. When Britain declared war with France and the colonial militia faced possible service in Canada, his Captain consulted a higher officer, who granted Burwell an exemption from duty. The document of exemption was preserved in the family. (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914)
narrative_blocks.body: When the children in the little schoolhouse on Phillida Hill laughed at his cleft-palate speech, Burwell refused to attend. He taught himself to read, write, and keep accounts, his spelling defiantly phonetic — if he chose to spell his principal crop “ri,” that was the best way to spell it.

narrative_blocks.header: Defended Kingston and the Continental Congress in 1777
narrative_blocks.detail: His service in both deployments was brief — not from cowardice but from the practical impossibility of the countersign problem. His unit could not rely on him for sentry duty or night patrol, since any stranger who challenged him could not understand his response. He served with what he could offer and was released. He is on the DAR lineage rolls through his descendant Miss Sarah Newton Church (NSDAR No. 99934; DAR Lineage Book 100:291). (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1777 Burwell was at Kingston, New York, when the Continental Congress was assembled there under threat from British troops. In 1779 he enlisted in Capt. Charles Norton's Company, Col. Thaddeus Cook's regiment, and was called to New Haven and Fairfield during the British coastal raids of July 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: His stammer nearly kept him from war
narrative_blocks.body: Born without a roof to his mouth, Burwell Newton could not give a military countersign clearly enough for strangers to understand — a danger that could get a sentry shot. When Britain’s war with France threatened to send the militia to Canada, Col. Timothy Stone authorized his discharge, writing that officers had “full Power to Relieve any Soldier… unfit for service.”

cross_connections.display_label: a great-great-grandson who overcame the same speech difficulty

military_service.notes: At the defence of Kingston, New York, 1777, when Continental Congress was assembled there and threatened by British troops. Called to New Haven and Fairfield in July 1779. Did not remain long in service — his speech impediment made it dangerous: strangers could not understand him and he risked being shot when unable to give the countersign intelligibly.

bio.bio_blurb: Durham farmer who soldiered despite a cleft palate

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained November 13, 1761. "Resided at Greenfield, where he was pastor of church for 53 years" per EH (actual span closer to 55 years, 1761-1816).

cross_connections.display_label: She left him a £30 Yale bequest he fulfilled in 1758

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1758 — Greenfield Massachusetts pastor 56 years
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at the First Church in Greenfield, Massachusetts on November 18, 1761. He served Greenfield for fifty-six years until his death in December 1816.

narrative_blocks.header: Aunt's £30 bequest set the ministry in motion
narrative_blocks.body: His aunt Alice Newton Buckingham left him £30 for college if he proved 'likely for public service' — he was barely three years old when she made the bequest. He graduated Yale eighteen years later and fulfilled her hope.

narrative_blocks.header: Town stayed a single parish all 56 years
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted Greenfield's call on the condition that all persons of 'competent knowledge and sober lives' be permitted communion, avoiding doctrinal narrowness. The entire town remained united in a single parish throughout his ministry — an unusual achievement in the fractious colonial church landscape.

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized an enslaved girl called Tinner in 1767
narrative_blocks.body: In his record of baptisms for September 20, 1767, he noted 'Tinner, my negro girl' — an enslaved person in his household. This stands in tension with his liberal communion policy and the harmony of his ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Diary: 'I would not live always'
narrative_blocks.body: On May 23, 1812 he wrote in his diary: 'I shall not and I would not live always.' He died four years later in December 1816, in his eightieth year, having outlived his wife by eleven years.

cross_connections.display_label: received Merwin into the Milford church, 1661

sources.notes: Miles Merwin and Platt connection documented

narrative_blocks.header: Received into Milford Church in 1661
narrative_blocks.body: On May 4, 1661, Miles Merwin was received into the Milford Church personally by Rev. Roger Newton, its founding pastor. His third wife Sarah Platt was a daughter of Deacon Richard Platt, another Milford founding settler — placing him at the intersection of Milford's most prominent founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughters were first cousins to the Platt
narrative_blocks.body: His third wife Sarah Platt was daughter of Deacon Richard Platt; Joseph Platt was Sarah's brother. Joseph's daughters Phebe and Hannah Platt each married a Newton first cousin. The Merwin daughters and Platt daughters were thus first cousins — all woven into the same tight circle of Milford families.

sources.notes: Listed as daughter of Richard Platt; marriages and death confirmed

death.cause: Died Milford shortly before Feb 13, 1684/5, when inventory of estate taken. Burial place unknown — probably in his orchard.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Milford Cemetery commemorates him. Actual burial place unknown; probably in his orchard per family tradition.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary (maiden name unknown), wife of George Platt
parents.father_research_notes: George Platt of Ware (bpt May 13, 1582; d. Apr 20, 1609) — tailor

migration.departure: England 1637-1638 — sailed with Rev. Peter Prudden's Hertfordshire company
migration.notes: Sold his lands and houses at Ware by final concord at Westminster Apr 25, 1638. Was among the 66 men who formed themselves into a church society at New Haven under Rev. Peter Prudden Aug 22, 1639, which group then settled Milford. Enrolled among first settlers of Milford Nov 20, 1639, with four in his family. Home lot #38: 4 acres 1 rod near present West Main and Cherry Streets, Milford.

church_roles.notes: Chosen deacon 1669. Witnessed the will of Rev. Peter Prudden.

cross_connections.display_label: Both came from Hertfordshire; both among Milford's Seven Pillars.
Adjoining home lots in the Hertfordshire Quarter, New Haven.
led the Cambridge circle Platt neighbored in Hertfordshire

notable_persons.name: Ben Affleck
notable_persons.description: Actor and director (Good Will Hunting, Batman v Superman, Gone Girl). Descended from Richard Platt through a daughter who married and lost the Platt surname — six generations documented on PBS Finding Your Roots (Season 3, October 2016), hosted by Henry Louis Gates Jr.
notable_persons.source: PBS Finding Your Roots Season 3; confirmed by Milford genealogist Richard Platt and Alvin Platt's 2003 Special Compilation of the History and Genealogy of Richard Platt
notable_persons.notes: Gates presented Affleck with six generations of Platt charts on air. The Milford Richard Platt Society confirmed the identification matches their documented records.

narrative_blocks.header: He left a Bible to each
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Richard Platt made his will in 1683, he bequeathed a Bible to each of his nineteen grandchildren — a gesture that, as one historian noted, "betrays a profound interest in the best things in life" in rough pioneer days. His estate was valued at approximately £600 sterling.

narrative_blocks.header: From Rev. Chauncey's congregation in Ware to founding
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Platt grew up in Ware, Hertfordshire — a hotbed of Puritanism where the famous Rev. Charles Chauncey (later president of Harvard) preached from 1627 to 1633. Deeply influenced by Chauncey's teaching, he came to New England in 1638 with Rev. Peter Prudden's Hertfordshire company, and became one of 66 founding members of the Milford church on August 22, 1639.

narrative_blocks.header: His name is on Milford's Founder's Bridge
narrative_blocks.body: The Platt stone on the Wepawaug Memorial Bridge in Milford reads: "Deacon Richard Platt, Obit 1684 — Mary His Wife." He was chosen Deacon of First Church Milford in 1669, witnessed the will of Rev. Peter Prudden, and appears as a witness in the colony records. He is buried in his orchard at lot #38, West Main and Cherry Streets, Milford.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children — their descendants married throughout
narrative_blocks.body: His nine children — Mary, John, Isaac, Samuel, Sarah, Epenetus, Hannah, Josiah, and Joseph — spread through the families of Milford and surrounding towns. Daughter Mary married Luke Atkinson; daughter Sarah married Thomas Beach then Miles Merwin; son Joseph married Mary Kellogg. Their descendants appear as spouses throughout the Newton-Hooker family network across three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: He left his entire estate to son Joseph
narrative_blocks.body: His will left Joseph Platt the entirety of his estate: "I give my son Joseph Platt all the remainder of my estate... I make my son Joseph Platt my executor." The family had emigrated from Hertfordshire, England in 1639, helping found New Haven and Milford. Joseph was the first Platt child born in America.

death.cause: Buried Milford March 24, 1675/6. Admitted to Milford church Aug 15, 1641.
